message.content:
Task id: T89 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Base commit: 352c1e5 (main) — FIRST run `git reset --hard 352c1e5` inside YOUR worktree (sanctioned base-refresh), then `git switch -c implement/T89`.

Go repo (wanbond). Gate: `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c`.

CONTEXT: T88 just landed the source->peer binding in internal/bind (demuxInbound: bound sources route to the bound peer's view; unbound sources are trial-decoded and bound ONLY by the first MAC-verified PROBE; unbound non-PROBE decodes `continue` the trial and drop at loop end). IMPORTANT: T88's demux only engages on sockets with >1 peer view — the <=1-view fast path dispatches everything (including unbound DATA) to the reader's own view, byte-identical single-peer behavior. Your task hardens the MULTI-PEER gate: verify (and where T88 left gaps, complete) that on a SHARED (multi-view) socket, DATA/PARITY from an unbound source NEVER reaches any peer's resequencer/fecRecv — regardless of which codec happens to garble-decode it — and that after an authenticated PROBE binds the source, subsequent DATA routes only to that peer. Much of this may already hold from T88 — your deliverable is the explicit acceptance coverage plus any gap-closing; do NOT change the single-peer fast path.

headline: Gate DATA/PARITY from unbound sources

description: In the demux path, drop DATA/PARITY arriving from a source with no established source->peer binding (never attribute it to any peer's resequencer/fecRecv). Once an authenticated PROBE binds the source, subsequent DATA/PARITY from it route to that peer. Rely on WG handshake/keepalive retransmit to cover the brief pre-binding gap.

acceptance: Test: DATA from an unbound source reaches NO resequencer (dropped) on a shared multi-view socket — including a DATA frame that VALIDLY decodes under some peer's codec (encode it under a real peer's psk); after an authenticated PROBE binds that source to peer B, subsequent DATA lands in B's resequencer only. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
8	)
9	
10	// Default WireGuard message-type discriminants and the keepalive size, mirrored from
11	// amneziawg-go/device (noise-protocol.go) rather than imported: the bind depends on the
12	// conn package only, and pulling the whole device package in for these constants would
13	// couple the transport to the engine's platform/init machinery. The type is the
14	// little-endian uint32 at the head of the message; the keepalive is a transport message
15	// with empty content — MessageTransportHeaderSize (16) + poly1305 tag (16). Kept in
16	// lockstep with device.Default*Type / device.MessageKeepaliveSize.
17	const (
18		wgMessageInitiationType  uint32 = 1 // handshake initiation (device.DefaultMessageInitiationType)
19		wgMessageResponseType    uint32 = 2 // handshake response (device.DefaultMessageResponseType)
20		wgMessageCookieReplyType uint32 = 3 // cookie reply (device.DefaultMessageCookieReplyType)
21		wgMessageTransportType   uint32 = 4 // transport data / keepalive (device.DefaultMessageTransportType)
22	
23		// wgTypeLen is the length of the leading little-endian uint32 message type.
24		wgTypeLen = 4
25		// wgKeepaliveSize is the on-wire size of a WireGuard keepalive (device.MessageKeepaliveSize
26		// == MessageTransportSize). AmneziaWG does NOT junk-prefix transport frames, so a
27		// keepalive stays this size under obfuscation; a transport frame longer than this
28		// carries tunnelled payload and is bulk data.
29		wgKeepaliveSize = 32
30		// wgMagicHeaderMin is amneziawg-go's threshold for a custom magic header taking effect:
31		// device.go applies a configured header only when it is > 4, otherwise the default type
32		// word stands (so a header of 1..4 is a no-op and the wire word is the default). The
33		// classifier mirrors that rule exactly so it matches what is actually on the wire.
34		wgMagicHeaderMin uint32 = 4
35	)
36	
37	// wgClassifier maps an outbound WireGuard datagram to its pacer traffic class (defect
38	// D22), parameterized by the tunnel's AmneziaWG obfuscation profile so classification is
39	// correct in advanced-security mode — wanbond's PRIMARY deployment — and not only in
40	// vanilla WireGuard. AmneziaWG breaks a hardcoded classifier two ways (amneziawg-go
41	// send.go): (a) custom magic headers h1..h4 replace the literal 1/2/3/4 type words, and
42	// (b) s1/s2 junk bytes are PREPENDED to handshake initiation/response datagrams, shifting
43	// the type word off offset 0. This classifier holds the CONFIGURED type words and junk
44	// prefix lengths and reads the type word at the right offset for each candidate type.
45	//
46	// A zero (unconfigured) profile yields the vanilla classifier: default type words, no
47	// junk prefix.
48	type wgClassifier struct {
49		initType      uint32 // effective handshake-initiation type word (h1, or default)
50		responseType  uint32 // effective handshake-response type word (h2, or default)
51		cookieType    uint32 // effective cookie-reply type word (h3, or default)
52		transportType uint32 // effective transport (data/keepalive) type word (h4, or default)
53		initJunk      int    // s1: junk bytes prepended before an initiation packet's type word
54		responseJunk  int    // s2: junk bytes prepended before a response packet's type word
55	}
56	
57	// newWGClassifier builds the classifier from the tunnel's Amnezia profile. It mirrors
58	// amneziawg-go's ">4 or default" magic-header rule (device.go) so the type words match
59	// the wire exactly, and takes s1/s2 as the initiation/response junk-prefix lengths. An
60	// unconfigured (zero-value) profile produces the vanilla classifier.
61	func newWGClassifier(a config.Amnezia) wgClassifier {
62		return wgClassifier{
63			initType:      effectiveMagic(a.H1, wgMessageInitiationType),
64			responseType:  effectiveMagic(a.H2, wgMessageResponseType),
65			cookieType:    effectiveMagic(a.H3, wgMessageCookieReplyType),
66			transportType: effectiveMagic(a.H4, wgMessageTransportType),
67			initJunk:      junkLen(a.S1),
68			responseJunk:  junkLen(a.S2),
69		}
70	}
71	
72	// effectiveMagic returns the type word actually placed on the wire for a header: the
73	// configured value when it exceeds wgMagicHeaderMin (amneziawg-go's activation rule),
74	// otherwise the default. This matches device.go, where a header of 1..4 is ignored.
75	func effectiveMagic(configured, def uint32) uint32 {
76		if configured > wgMagicHeaderMin {
77			return configured
78		}
79		return def
80	}
81	
82	// junkLen clamps a junk-prefix length to a non-negative offset (a zero/omitted profile
83	// has no prefix; config validation forbids a negative value).
84	func junkLen(s int) int {
85		if s < 0 {
86			return 0
87		}
88		return s
89	}
90	
91	// classify maps one outbound WireGuard message to its pacer traffic class. Order matters
92	// for the safety property "DATA never classifies as control": a transport frame leads
93	// with the transport type word at offset 0 and is checked FIRST, so a bulk data frame
94	// (transport, size > keepalive) always resolves to ClassData before any control branch is
95	// considered. Handshake initiation/response type words are read at their junk-shifted
96	// offsets (s1/s2). A frame that matches nothing — including AmneziaWG junk packets — is
97	// data (fail toward pacing; an unrecognised frame must not gain the control-class bypass).
98	func (c wgClassifier) classify(pkt []byte) sched.FrameClass {
99		// Transport (data or keepalive): type word at offset 0, never junk-prefixed. Only a
100		// keepalive-sized transport is a control frame; anything larger is tunnelled bulk data.
101		if t, ok := readType(pkt, 0); ok && t == c.transportType {
102			if len(pkt) == wgKeepaliveSize {
103				return sched.ClassControl
104			}
105			return sched.ClassData
106		}
107		// Cookie reply: type word at offset 0, not junk-prefixed.
108		if t, ok := readType(pkt, 0); ok && t == c.cookieType {
109			return sched.ClassControl
110		}
111		// Handshake initiation: type word after s1 junk bytes.
112		if t, ok := readType(pkt, c.initJunk); ok && t == c.initType {
113			return sched.ClassControl
114		}
115		// Handshake response: type word after s2 junk bytes.
116		if t, ok := readType(pkt, c.responseJunk); ok && t == c.responseType {
117			return sched.ClassControl
118		}
119		return sched.ClassData
120	}
121	
122	// classifyBatch returns the pacer traffic class for a batch of WireGuard datagrams handed
123	// to Send in one call. The batch is classified ClassControl when ANY buffer is a control
124	// frame: the batch shares one Pick (one path selection / one pacing decision), so biasing
125	// a mixed batch toward the control class keeps a co-batched handshake/keepalive out of the
126	// shed set — the conservative, rekey-favouring choice. In practice WireGuard emits control
127	// frames in their own Send calls, so a mixed batch is rare. An empty batch is data.
128	func (c wgClassifier) classifyBatch(bufs [][]byte) sched.FrameClass {
129		for _, b := range bufs {
130			if c.classify(b) == sched.ClassControl {
131				return sched.ClassControl
132			}
133		}
134		return sched.ClassData
135	}
136	
137	// readType reads the little-endian uint32 message-type word at byte offset off, reporting
138	// ok=false when the buffer is too short to hold a type word at that offset.
139	func readType(pkt []byte, off int) (uint32, bool) {
140		if off < 0 || off+wgTypeLen > len(pkt) {
141			return 0, false
142		}
143		return binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(pkt[off : off+wgTypeLen]), true
144	}
145

content.content:
1075	// readLoop reaches it through demuxInbound, which first resolves the owning view on a shared
1076	// concentrator socket (on the single-peer edge/hub the reader's own view is the owner, so
1077	// demuxInbound's fast path calls this directly — byte-identical to the pre-concentrator
1078	// behaviour). Delivery up the WG
1079	// path is deferred to the resequencer (Pop, in the engine-facing drainer): a DATA
1080	// frame is not handed up here but pushed into the shared resequencer to be released in
1081	// outer-seq order.
1082	//
1083	//   - DATA: the decoded inner datagram is pushed into the resequencer keyed by
1084	//     the frame's outer-seq (delivered later, in order, via Pop). The path's
1085	//     remote is NOT learned here — remote-learning is authenticated-only (see
1086	//     below), so a forged DATA frame cannot steer a path's return endpoint.
1087	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=false: an authenticated peer probe. Its source is learned as
1088	//     the path's remote (D11: a probe-only backup path gets a usable return remote
1089	//     before any DATA flows) and it is reflected straight back to that source via
1090	//     this path's socket (T13 Reflector). Reflection writes independently of the
1091	//     scheduler/getRemote so an echo returns even on a not-yet-active path.
1092	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=true: an authenticated echo of one of our own probes. Its
1093	//     source is learned as the remote too, and the raw echo is fed into this
1094	//     path's Prober (HandleEcho) to update RTT/loss and drive liveness.
1095	//   - anything else (PARITY/CONTROL/malformed): dropped.
1096	//
1097	// Remote-learning and reflection touch only authenticated (MAC-verified) PROBE
1098	// frames — Decode has already verified the tag for the PROBE kind — which is what
1099	// resolves D9: an attacker who forges an unauthenticated DATA frame can no longer
1100	// repoint a path's return endpoint.
1101	//
1102	// FEC seam (T24): DATA ingestion via resequencer.Observe is keyed purely on
1103	// outer-seq, so an FEC decoder can slot in BEFORE the resequencer with no change
1104	// here — PARITY (dropped today) will feed the decoder, which reconstructs missing
1105	// DATA frames and calls Observe with their ORIGINAL outer-seq, identical to a
1106	// natively-received frame.
1107	func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1108		fr, err := ps.codec.Decode(raw)
1109		if err != nil {
1110			return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
1111		}
1112		m.dispatchInbound(ps, fr, raw, srcAP)
1113	}
1114	
1115	// demuxInbound is the readLoop's per-frame entry on a (possibly shared) socket: it resolves
1116	// which bound peer's view owns the datagram, then hands the frame to handleInbound on THAT
1117	// view (T88). Because the readLoop holds only the PRIMARY's view of a shared socket (one
1118	// reader per socket), the routing that a concentrator needs — a datagram from peer B decoded
1119	// and resequenced under peer B's plane — happens HERE, not by trusting the reader's own view.
1120	//
1121	// Fast path: a socket with a single peer view (the edge/hub, or any socket not shared by a
1122	// second peer) needs no demux — dispatch on the reader's own view, byte-identical to the
1123	// pre-concentrator behaviour. The per-socket view snapshot is read lock-free; a nil/one-entry
1124	// snapshot means "not a shared concentrator socket".
1125	//
1126	// Shared socket (>1 view):
1127	//   - A source already bound by a prior authenticated PROBE routes straight to that peer's
1128	//     view of THIS socket (one lock-free map Load). A frame that does not verify under that
1129	//     peer's psk — a spoofed source it cannot forge the MAC for — is dropped by handleInbound.
1130	//   - An unbound source is trial-decoded against each peer's psk-derived codec (O(peers),
1131	//     bounded by the static peer count). Only an authenticated PROBE establishes a binding
1132	//     (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned only from authenticated PROBEs), and a
1133	//     PROBE's MAC verifies under EXACTLY ONE psk — so the FIRST psk whose codec yields a PROBE
1134	//     identifies the peer, binds the source, dispatches, and the loop STOPS there. A non-PROBE
1135	//     decode does NOT stop the trial: DATA/PARITY carry no MAC and are forgeable by design, so
1136	//     a genuine PROBE from a later peer can cross-psk-garble into a DATA/PARITY kind under an
1137	//     earlier peer's codec (~0.4%); the loop must therefore `continue` past a non-PROBE decode
1138	//     to give every remaining psk its chance to authenticate a PROBE. A non-PROBE decode
1139	//     carries no binding authority and is dropped either way — a genuine DATA/PARITY from a
1140	//     not-yet-bound source never dispatches or binds until that peer's PROBE binds it, so
1141	//     continuing past it changes nothing for genuine frames. A forged/garbage frame verifies
1142	//     as a PROBE under NO psk, binds nothing, and is dropped cheaply.
1143	func (m *Multipath) demuxInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1144		views := ps.views.Load()
1145		if views == nil || len(*views) <= 1 {
1146			m.handleInbound(ps, raw, srcAP)
1147			return
1148		}
1149		if bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(srcAP.Addr()); ok {
1150			for _, v := range *views {
1151				if v.peer == bound {
1152					m.handleInbound(v, raw, srcAP)
1153					return
1154				}
1155			}
1156			return // the bound peer holds no view of this socket (removed): drop
1157		}
1158		for _, v := range *views {
1159			fr, err := v.codec.Decode(raw)
1160			if err != nil {
1161				continue // not this peer's psk — try the next
1162			}
1163			if _, isProbe := fr.(frame.Probe); !isProbe {
1164				// Decoded under this psk but not a PROBE (DATA/PARITY carry no MAC, so
1165				// this may be a genuine PROBE from a later peer that cross-garbled into an
1166				// unauthenticated kind here). No binding authority: try the remaining psks
1167				// rather than aborting — a genuine unbound DATA/PARITY still never binds.
1168				continue
1169			}
1170			m.bindSourceToPeer(srcAP.Addr(), v.peer)
1171			m.dispatchInbound(v, fr, raw, srcAP) // already decoded: dispatch without re-decoding
1172			return
1173		}
1174		// No peer's psk verified: a forged/garbage frame. No binding, drop.
1175	}
1176	
1177	// lookupPeerBySource resolves a learned source address to the peer bound to it, or nil when
1178	// the source is not yet bound. It reads the copy-on-write binding map with a single lock-free
1179	// Load, so the concentrator receive hot path never takes m.mu (see peerBySource).
1180	func (m *Multipath) lookupPeerBySource(addr netip.Addr) (*peerState, bool) {
1181		mp := m.peerBySource.Load()
1182		if mp == nil {
1183			return nil, false
1184		}
1185		p, ok := (*mp)[addr]
1186		return p, ok
1187	}
1188	
1189	// bindSourceToPeer records addr→p in the source-demux map, installed lock-free by a CAS
1190	// republish of a copy with the entry added (T88). It takes NO lock: a reader must never block
1191	// on m.mu (Close waits on the readers WHILE holding m.mu), so the binding — written from a
1192	// readLoop goroutine — must not acquire it. Idempotent: an already-present addr→p binding is a
1193	// no-op, and a lost CAS (a concurrent bind on another socket) simply retries. Copy-on-write
1194	// keeps every published map immutable, so a concurrent lookupPeerBySource over the old snapshot
1195	// is never disturbed.
1196	func (m *Multipath) bindSourceToPeer(addr netip.Addr, p *peerState) {
1197		for {
1198			old := m.peerBySource.Load()
1199			if old != nil {
1200				if existing, ok := (*old)[addr]; ok && existing == p {
1201					return
1202				}
1203			}
1204			var n int
1205			if old != nil {
1206				n = len(*old)
1207			}
1208			next := make(map[netip.Addr]*peerState, n+1)
1209			if old != nil {
1210				for k, v := range *old {
1211					next[k] = v
1212				}
1213			}
1214			next[addr] = p
1215			if m.peerBySource.CompareAndSwap(old, &next) {
1216				return
1217			}
1218			// Lost the race with a concurrent bind: reload and retry.
1219		}
1220	}
1221	
1222	// dispatchInbound handles one already-decoded inbound frame on the resolved peer's view (ps):
1223	// it routes to that peer's resequencer / FEC decoder / reflector. The source demux in
1224	// demuxInbound has already selected ps so a shared socket serving many peers resequences each
1225	// peer's stream against that peer's own buffer; on the single-peer edge/hub ps.peer is the
1226	// embedded primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton. raw is retained for
1227	// the probe transport (HandleEcho / Reflect re-decode it under the peer's psk).
1228	func (m *Multipath) dispatchInbound(ps *peerPathState, fr frame.Frame, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1229		pr := ps.peer
1230		switch f := fr.(type) {
1231		case frame.Data:
1232			// Decode already returned a fresh copy of the payload (it aliases nothing
1233			// else), so the resequencer may take ownership of it directly.
1234			rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1235			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1236				// FEC on (T24): offer the data shard to the decoder BEFORE resequencing so a
1237				// later parity frame can reconstruct any group-mate lost in transit, then
1238				// deliver THIS received frame in its own right (the decoder never echoes a
1239				// directly-received data shard back). The shard's coded bytes are
1240				// OuterSeq || Payload — the same bytes the sender coded parity over.
1241				shard := fec.DataShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.FECIndex), Payload: fecShardPayload(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload)}
1242				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1243				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1244				// Residual-loss accounting (T29): this outer-seq was natively delivered, so mark
1245				// it present in the post-recovery loss estimator. A seq never marked here nor via
1246				// a reconstruction below is loss that FEC did not mask.
1247				if fr.connLoss != nil {
1248					fr.connLoss.Observe(f.OuterSeq)
1249				}
1250				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1251			} else {
1252				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1253			}
1254		case frame.Parity:
1255			// PARITY feeds the FEC decoder (T24); a group that has now accumulated enough
1256			// shards reconstructs its missing data frames, each resequenced at its ORIGINAL
1257			// outer-seq (carried in the recovered shard's coded bytes) so recovery composes
1258			// with T18 exactly like a natively-received frame. With FEC off, PARITY is
1259			// dropped (the pre-T24 behaviour).
1260			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1261				shard := fec.ParityShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.ParityIndex), DataCount: int(f.DataCount), Payload: f.Payload}
1262				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1263				m.observeRecovered(fr, pr.resequencer.Load(), recovered, srcAP)
1264			}
1265		case frame.Probe:
1266			// Authenticated (the PROBE MAC verified in Decode): learn the return remote
1267			// from it, below the engine's virtual endpoint (no roaming churn).
1268			ps.setRemote(srcAP)
1269			if f.IsEcho {
1270				if ps.prober != nil {
1271					// A replay/forgery/wrong-path echo is rejected inside HandleEcho and
1272					// leaves liveness untouched; the error is a per-frame drop, not fatal.
1273					// ps.prober is the path's OWN immutable prober — never a lookup into a
1274					// dynamically-mutated slice — so runtime add/remove cannot race this.
1275					_ = ps.prober.HandleEcho(raw)
1276				}
1277				return
1278			}
1279			if pr.reflector != nil {
1280				if echo, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
1281					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe, so this receive-goroutine reflection
1282					// races no in-flight Send on the same socket.
1283					_, _ = ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(echo, srcAP)
1284				}
1285			}
1286		default:
1287			// CONTROL (and any unhandled kind) is not delivered up this path.
1288		}
1289	}
1290	
1291	// observeRecovered feeds every FEC-reconstructed data frame into the resequencer at
1292	// its ORIGINAL outer-seq (recovered from the shard's coded bytes) via the NON-resyncing
1293	// ObserveRecovered path, so a frame rebuilt from parity resequences identically to a
1294	// natively-received frame — filling the exact gap the resequencer would otherwise time

120		// appendBody appends the plaintext body (kind byte || header || payload).

122		isFrame()
123	}
124	
125	// Data wraps a single opaque WireGuard datagram. OuterSeq is this codec's own
126	// sequence space (never the inner WG counter); PathID/FECGroup/FECIndex/Flags feed
127	// the multipath scheduler (T12), FEC (T14/T24), and resequencer (T18). FECIndex is
128	// the data shard's position within its FEC group (0..M-1); it is 0 and inert when
129	// FEC is disabled. The receiver reconstructs the FEC data-shard bytes as
130	// OuterSeq || Payload, so a shard recovered from parity carries its own outer-seq
131	// (T24) — no separate per-group base state is needed to resequence a recovered
132	// frame.
133	type Data struct {
134		OuterSeq uint64
135		PathID   uint8
136		FECGroup uint32
137		FECIndex uint8
138		Flags    uint8
139		Payload  []byte
140	}
141	
142	// Parity carries one FEC parity symbol for a fec-group. DataCount is the group
143	// cardinality M (the number of data shards the parity protects); the decoder learns
144	// M from any surviving parity frame, so it must ride the wire (T24).
145	type Parity struct {
146		FECGroup    uint32
147		ParityIndex uint16
148		DataCount   uint8
149		PathID      uint8
150		Payload     []byte
151	}
152	
153	// Probe is an authenticated path probe used for RTT and liveness estimation.
154	//
155	// IsEcho is the request/response discriminant. A probe emitted by an originator
156	// (telemetry.Prober.SendProbe) carries IsEcho=false; the responder reflects it
157	// verbatim with IsEcho=true (telemetry.Reflector.Reflect). The two are otherwise
158	// byte-for-body-identical, so the receiving transport needs this bit to route an
159	// inbound frame: IsEcho=false is a peer probe to REFLECT, IsEcho=true is an echo

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
test(bind): T89 — gate DATA/PARITY from unbound sources on shared sockets

Add explicit acceptance coverage for the T88 multi-peer source->peer demux
gate: on a shared (>1 view) concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY from a source
with no established binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — even when the
frame VALIDLY decodes under a real peer's psk (a MAC-less DATA/PARITY body
deciphers cleanly, but carries no binding authority). After an authenticated
PROBE binds the source to peer B, subsequent DATA routes to B's resequencer
only.

Verified discriminating by mutation: dispatching the non-PROBE trial-decode
(instead of the T88 continue-then-drop) makes the unbound DATA leak into peer
B's resequencer and fails the test. No production code changed — the gate
already holds from T88; the single-peer fast path is untouched.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git rev-parse HEAD
input.description: Commit the new test on the task branch

### Session summary
- **Did:** implemented task T89 in worktree `implement/T89` — added explicit acceptance coverage for the multi-peer unbound-source DATA/PARITY gate.
- **Achieved:** pass; resultCommit `e876ba08d7e4448fb0cd11bd5467c5fff2c2c7f3`; full gate green (`go build`, `go vet`, `go test -race ./internal/bind/...`, `go test ./...` all clean).
- **Discovered:** T88's `demuxInbound` already fully implements the gate — an unbound source's DATA/PARITY that validly decodes under a peer's psk hits the `!isProbe` → `continue` path and drops at loop end (carries no binding authority). No production-code gap remained; the deliverable is the test. Confirmed the test is discriminating via a mutation (dispatching the non-PROBE decode makes the unbound DATA leak into peer B's resequencer and the test fails).
- **Issues:** none. Single-peer fast path untouched; no behavior change so no docs-sync needed.
